oob.text:
I'll research this thoroughly. A pergola sounds simple, but Portland has specific rules that depend on size, location on your lot, and your zoning — and the answer "do I need a permit" hinges on those details. Let me dig in properly.I have the general framework. Now I need to know the specific zoning for the user's property — that determines setbacks, lot coverage limits, and floor area ratio. Let me look up the address on PortlandMaps.Good — I have property facts (4,792 sq ft lot, 810 sq ft 2-bed/2-bath built 1988). Now let me look up the actual zoning from PortlandMaps.Let me try to pull the property's zoning directly from PortlandMaps.The neighborhood mostly shows R5 zoning, with some R2.5 nearby. Let me get the exact zoning for the user's property.I have the property ID format now. Let me pull the user's property record directly.Let me try the city's open data ArcGIS API to get zoning programmatically.Let me get more specific on how Portland treats pergolas — open-roof vs. covered makes a big difference, and so does attached vs. freestanding.Now I have the key distinction clear: open pergolas (just posts and beams) are classified differently from "covered" pergolas. Let me confirm the open-pergola rules, and try one more approach to identify zoning at this exact address.I have everything I need now. Let me write this up. (One note: I tried to pull your exact zoning from PortlandMaps directly but their site didn't return data through my search tools; I'll point you to where to verify it yourself in two clicks.)

---

**Short answer: yes, almost certainly. The design choices you make up front determine whether you can skip a permit entirely or need to file one.**

## The single most useful distinction

Portland classifies pergolas two different ways under Zoning Code 33.110.245, and the rules diverge sharply:

An **open pergola** (posts plus rafters, slats, lattice, or trellis material with no solid roof) is a *"detached uncovered vertical structure"*; the same category as trellises and arbors. Lightly regulated. It does not count toward your building coverage (which only counts impervious overhead structures), and it does not count toward floor area ratio (FAR).

A **covered pergola** (polycarbonate panels, fabric canopy, louvered aluminum that closes, shingled mini-roof) is a *"detached covered accessory structure"*; the same category as a shed or carport. It triggers building coverage and the 20-ft height cap, and you'll need to keep it inside the buildable area unless it qualifies for a setback exception.

This is the lever most homeowners don't realize they're pulling.

## The permit-free thresholds (Oregon state code, applied by Portland)

For a freestanding pergola: no building permit if it's ≤ 200 sq ft and ≤ 15 ft tall.

For a pergola attached to your house and covering a patio: no building permit if it's ≤ 200 sq ft, average roof height ≤ 12 ft, and at least 3 ft from any property line.

200 sq ft is roughly 14×14 or 12×16; plenty for a dining-and-lounging space. Important caveat: skipping the *building* permit doesn't skip *zoning*. Setbacks, building coverage, and FAR still apply, and you may still need a small zoning permit if the structure changes the building footprint.

## Setbacks for your property

R5 zone (the most common designation around your address) typically requires 10 ft front, 5 ft side, 5 ft rear for primary structures, and accessory structures can sometimes go closer to side and rear lot lines if certain conditions are met. R2.5 (also present in your area) is more permissive.

A useful loophole: an *open* pergola counted as an "uncovered vertical structure" is allowed inside required building setbacks if it's no larger than 3 ft in width/depth and no taller than 8 ft. So a slim, traditional pergola can hug a fence line. Bigger than that and the full setback rules apply. There's also a specific carve-out allowing a single arbor up to 6 ft wide, 3 ft deep, and 8 ft tall in the front setback (probably not what you want, but good to know).

## Your specific situation, things to flag

Your lot is 4,792 sq ft, just under the 5,000 sq ft R5 standard, so the FAR math is tight. The 2022 listing for your home mentions a "new backyard shed" already on the property; that shed counts as an existing accessory structure and eats into both the 20% accessory-structure-coverage cap and your overall FAR allowance. Worth measuring before you size the pergola.

Also worth knowing: there's a one-time 250 sq ft FAR exception per 5-year period for properties whose primary structure passed final inspection at least 5 years ago. Your house was built in 1988, so this is available if you ever need it for something more substantial.

## Verify your exact zoning before committing

I tried to pull your zoning directly but couldn't reach the live data from where I sit. The two-click verification: go to **portlandmaps.com**, search "4773 SE 52nd Ave", and click "Permits & Zoning" → "Zoning & Districts". You're looking for the base zone (likely R5 or R2.5) and any overlay letters (like "a", "d", or others). Overlays can change setback or design rules. While you're there, also check for any environmental overlay (rare on this block but worth a glance) and any "z" constrained-sites overlay.

## Above-and-beyond bits

**Free 15-minute zoning consultation.** Portland Permitting & Development offers a free 15-minute appointment with a planner who can confirm your zoning, setbacks, and whether your specific design needs a permit. Call 503-823-7300 to schedule. This is the single highest-leverage thing you can do; it's free and will save you from any surprises. They'll also tell you whether your block has any plan-district quirks.

**Design hack if you want maximum flexibility.** Build it open (no impervious roof). You get shade from rafter spacing, climbing plants, or a removable shade cloth, while the structure stays in the lightly-regulated category. You can always add a removable canopy later for events. This keeps it out of building-coverage and FAR math entirely.

**HOA / CC&Rs.** Your property is in Mt. Scott-Arleta and doesn't appear to have an HOA based on typical patterns for that neighborhood, but check your title docs for any recorded covenants from the original 1988 subdivision. Permit-free at the city level doesn't mean permit-free at the deed level.

**Practical sequence.** Verify zoning on PortlandMaps, measure existing shed footprint, sketch a 12×16 or 14×14 open pergola sited at least 5 ft from side/rear lot lines, then call PP&D for the free 15-min consult to confirm. If everything checks out, you can build without a building permit; just keep it open-roof and within the thresholds above.

If you want, share what PortlandMaps shows for the zoning and I can tighten the setback numbers and confirm the exact FAR/building-coverage math for your situation.

mcp.text:
I have everything I need. Let me build you a visual of your property with the buildable zone, then give you the full answer.Yes, you can put up a pergola. Your property is in good shape for it: zoned **R5 (Residential 5,000)**, no historic district, no overlay zones, no environmental constraints, no plan district. The path is clean. Here's what your lot looks like with the setbacks applied:## The rules, in plain English

**Setbacks (how far from property lines):**
- 10 ft from SE 52nd Ave (front)
- 5 ft from each side property line
- 5 ft from the rear property line

**Do you need a building permit?** Probably not, if the pergola is:
- 200 sqft or smaller in footprint, AND
- 15 ft or shorter in height, AND
- non-habitable and detached

That covers the typical 12×16 or 10×12 backyard pergola. Bigger or taller and you're into a building permit. Either way, you still need to obey the setbacks — the permit exemption and the zoning rules are separate things.

**Important nuance most people miss: "covered" vs "uncovered" matters a lot.**

A traditional pergola with an open-slat / lattice top is classified as a *detached uncovered vertical structure* (Portland code calls these "trellises, arbors and other garden structures"). A pergola with a solid roof, polycarbonate panels, or a fixed fabric canopy is treated as a *detached covered accessory structure* — stricter rules, more likely to count as impervious surface for stormwater. If you want the open beam look, you have more freedom. If you want shade you can sit under in a downpour, expect more scrutiny.

## Things to check before you buy lumber

A few items that aren't obvious from "can I build a pergola" but will save you headaches:

**Your tree history.** A tree removal permit was applied for at this address in 2016 but expired without a final inspection (application 2016-176242). Portland's tree code (Title 11) requires lots to maintain a minimum tree density and treats removed-but-not-replaced trees as an outstanding obligation. If you dig footings near where a tree used to be, or the pergola location intersects an existing tree's root protection zone, that record could resurface. Worth a 5-minute phone call to Urban Forestry (503-823-8733) to see if anything's still flagged before you commit to a location.

**Building coverage and FAR.** Your house is 810 sqft on a 4,792 sqft lot — comfortably under R5's caps. A pergola with an open top doesn't add to building coverage (no impervious roof), but a solid-roofed one would. You're nowhere near limits either way.

**Soil and anchoring.** Portland Maps shows your soil as well-drained, hydrologic group B, with bedrock at 6 ft. Translation: standard concrete pier footings (think Sonotube + post anchor) will work fine. No special engineering needed for a typical pergola.

**Stormwater.** You're in the Taggart B/C combined sewer basin. An open pergola is invisible to stormwater rules. A roofed one would need to drain to a permeable area, not the gutter system, if it's over 200 sqft.

**The free expert call.** Portland Permitting & Development offers free 15-minute appointments with a planner. For a project this size it's overkill, but if you're hesitating between "open lattice" and "solid roof" or want certainty about the setbacks for a corner of the lot, it's the highest-leverage 15 minutes you can spend: [portland.gov/ppd](https://www.portland.gov/ppd) → "15-Minute Question Appointment."

## Practical next steps

1. **Pick the size.** Stay under 200 sqft and 15 ft tall to skip the building permit. A 12×16 (192 sqft) hits the sweet spot.
2. **Mark the corners with stakes** at least 5 ft from any side/rear property line and 10 ft from SE 52nd. If you're not sure exactly where your property lines are, a fence line is *not* a reliable proxy — Portland recommends a licensed surveyor for any work near the line.
3. **Decide covered or uncovered** before you order materials; it changes which rules apply.
4. **Pull the brochure.** Portland's [Brochure 3: Fences, Decks and Outdoor Residential Projects](https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/b3-residential-fences-decks-outdoor-projects) is the official one-stop reference. It's the source I'd trust over any contractor's "I think it's fine."

enhanced.text:
I'll dig into this properly for you. Since you gave me an actual address, I'm going to look up the real property data through Portland's official systems first, then verify the current pergola rules through Portland's code, rather than guessing from neighborhood patterns. Give me a few minutes.Now let me verify the current pergola permit rules from Portland's official sources:I have everything I need. Let me load the diagram module so I can draw you a visual of your lot's setback rules, then put it all together.Here's the answer, in plain English first, then the details.

## The short version

Yes, you can almost certainly put up a pergola at 4773 SE 52nd Ave. Your property is in a regular single-family residential zone called **R5**, with no historic, environmental, or design overlays to worry about (which is great news; those are the things that complicate small projects). For a typical backyard pergola that is freestanding, no taller than 15 feet, smaller than 200 square feet, kept at least 5 feet from your side and back property lines, and at least 10 feet from your front property line on SE 52nd Ave, you generally do not need a building permit, though you may still need a quick zoning permit. Once you start attaching it to the house, going bigger or taller, adding a solid roof, or putting it in your front yard, the rules get stricter and you'll likely need permits.

Let me show you what your lot looks like in terms of the rules, then walk through everything else.Now the full walkthrough.

## What's specific to your property

I pulled your property up on Portland's official **PortlandMaps** system (portlandmaps.com), which is the city's authoritative database. Here is what it says, in plain language:

Your lot sits in the **R5 zone** (full name: "Residential 5,000," which means single-family neighborhoods with minimum lots around 5,000 square feet). The city actually classifies your block as part of the **Woodstock** neighborhood, not Foster-Powell; SE 52nd Ave is the boundary between the two, and the city's records put you on the Woodstock side, though most people in the area use the names loosely. This doesn't change any of the rules; it's just a useful little fact.

Your lot is about **50 feet wide by 100 feet deep**, so right around 4,800 square feet (a classic Portland city lot). Your home (built in 1988) is 810 square feet, leaving you a lot of yard to work with. The front of the property faces west onto SE 52nd Ave, and the lot extends 100 feet east into the block, so your back yard is on the east side.

The really good news: **your property has no overlay zones**. Overlays are extra layers of rules that get stacked on top of the base zone. The most common ones in Portland are:

A **historic district** or **conservation district** would mean you'd need design review for almost anything visible from the street. Yours has none. An **environmental overlay** (often labeled "c" or "p") would protect natural resources like streams, wetlands, or steep slopes; yours has none. A **design overlay** (a "d" zone) would require design review even outside historic areas; yours has none. A **plan district** (like Eastmoreland or Laurelhurst) would add neighborhood-specific rules; yours has none. None of those apply to you, which makes a pergola project simpler than it would be in many parts of Portland.

A few other things worth knowing that I dug up on your specific lot. Your property has **no flood hazard, no landslide hazard, and no steep slope concerns**, so soil and drainage rules will be straightforward. There is **no heritage tree** registered on your lot. There is, however, a **2016 tree permit** on file at your address (a removal-with-replanting permit, now expired), which suggests a tree was removed and a replacement was planted somewhere on the property; if the replacement is still alive and is now 12 inches across or bigger, the tree code rules I describe below could apply to it. Your sewer lateral connects from the south side (the line runs in SE Long Street, with a branch coming up to your house roughly 10 feet underground), and the city's records show one **sewer backup history** at the address, so you'll want to know roughly where that line runs before you set any post holes (more on this in the "gotchas" section).

## Permits — what you need and what triggers them

The very condensed version: a small open backyard pergola usually slips under both permit thresholds. The moment it gets bigger, taller, attached, or covered, you need permits.

Two different rule books apply to your project, and they ask different questions.

The **building code** (Portland City Code Title 24, which is the city adopting the **Oregon Residential Specialty Code**) cares about whether the structure is safe — wind loads, post anchoring, that sort of thing. Under that code, you do not need a building permit for a freestanding (detached) pergola that is one story, has a footprint of 200 square feet or less, is no taller than 15 feet measured to the average roof height, and sits at least 3 feet from any property line. For a pergola that is **attached** to your house (bolted to the wall with a ledger board), the threshold is similar but slightly different: 200 square feet or less, 12-foot average roof height or less, and 3 feet or more from any property line. (Reference: Building Official Determination 21-05 and BOD 21-01, which apply Oregon Residential Specialty Code section R105.2.)

The **zoning code** (Portland City Code Title 33, specifically Chapter 33.110 for single-family zones) cares about *where* the structure sits and *how big* it is in proportion to the lot. Even if you are exempt from the building permit, **you may still need a zoning permit** to confirm your pergola meets setbacks, height, and lot coverage rules. The city is straightforward about this; PP&D (Portland Permitting & Development, the bureau that handles these reviews; renamed from BDS, the Bureau of Development Services, in July 2024) treats the zoning permit as a quick check rather than a full plan review.

A simple way to think about the lines you might cross:

If your pergola is **bigger than 200 square feet** of footprint, you cross into building permit territory. (PCC Title 24; ORSC R105.2.) About 14 feet by 14 feet is roughly 200 square feet, so plan around that if you want to stay exempt.

If your pergola is **taller than 15 feet** (detached) or 12 feet average roof height (attached patio cover), same answer — building permit. (PCC Title 24.)

If you **attach the pergola to your house**, even a small one, the city looks at the connection point more carefully because it affects your house's framing and waterproofing. Small attached pergolas can still be permit-exempt, but the threshold is lower (12-foot average roof height instead of 15), and inspectors are more likely to want to see how you attached it.

If you **add a solid roof** (real roofing material instead of open beams or slats), it stops being a pergola in the city's eyes and starts being a "patio cover," which has its own rules under Title 24 and under PCC 33.110.245 (detached and connected covered accessory structures). Solid roofs also create new impervious area, which has stormwater consequences.

If you build it in your **front yard** (the area between the house and SE 52nd Ave), the city is much pickier. Front yards have to stay relatively open in single-family zones, and small accessory structures generally can't be in the front 10 feet at all. (PCC 33.110.220 and 33.110.245.)

If your pergola goes **over a deck that's more than 30 inches off the ground**, the deck itself triggers a building permit regardless of the pergola, so the whole thing gets permitted together. (Oregon Residential Specialty Code; this is consistent across Oregon.)

For most regular backyard pergolas in your situation, you'll either need no permits, or just a quick zoning permit. Plan for a building permit if you cross any of the lines above.

## Trees

Portland is serious about trees. The rule book is **Title 11**, and it's run by **Urban Forestry** (a team that used to live under Portland Parks & Recreation and is now mostly merged into PP&D as part of the 2024 reorganization, though tree expertise still sits in Urban Forestry within Parks).

Two situations matter for your pergola.

**If you're not disturbing any tree's roots**, you generally don't have to do anything for trees. "Not disturbing roots" means no digging, no construction staging, no driving heavy equipment within the **root protection zone** of any regulated tree. The root protection zone is roughly 1 foot of radius for every inch of trunk diameter; so a 12-inch tree has a 12-foot RPZ around it.

**If your pergola posts will be in the root zone of a sizable tree**, you cross into Title 11 territory. The threshold is set in PCC 11.50.020 and 11.50.030: you need to deal with the tree code if your project disturbs more than 100 square feet within the root protection zone of a **private tree 12 inches or bigger** in diameter (measured at breast height, abbreviated DBH), or a **City tree 6 inches or bigger**, or a **street tree 3 inches or bigger**.

A **regulated tree** in plain English is any tree the city tracks. **Street trees** are the ones in the planting strip between sidewalk and curb (the city basically owns them even though they're on your frontage). **City trees** are on city-owned property, like in the right-of-way or in parks. **Private trees** are everything else on your lot. Heritage trees are a separate, more protected category; you don't have any.

Practical advice: if there's a mature tree anywhere near where you're thinking of putting the pergola, get out a tape measure and check the trunk diameter at about 4.5 feet off the ground (that's "breast height"). If it's 12 inches or more, sketch a circle around it with a radius equal to the diameter in feet. If your pergola posts fall in that circle, you'll likely need a tree review as part of your permit, which Urban Forestry handles. **The 2016 tree permit** I found on your property suggests a tree was planted there about nine years ago — depending on species, that tree might or might not have hit the 12-inch threshold yet. Worth a quick measurement before you start.

If a tree needs to be removed for the pergola, that's a separate Tree Permit under PCC 11.40, and Urban Forestry has to approve it. Removing a regulated tree without a permit is one of the more expensive mistakes you can make in Portland; mitigation fees run around $472 per inch of trunk diameter, and there are daily penalty options too. Always call Urban Forestry first if there's any tree question.

## Setbacks, lot coverage, and yard rules

Your zoning code uses the word **setback** to mean "the strip along each property line where you're not allowed to build." For your R5 lot, the rules from Title 33 Table 110-3 are:

**10 feet from the front property line** (the line along SE 52nd Ave). (PCC 33.110.220, Table 110-3.) Your pergola has to sit at least that far back from the sidewalk side of your lot.

**5 feet from each side property line** (north and south). (Same table.)

**5 feet from the rear property line** (the back of your lot). (Same table.)

**Lot coverage** is the share of your lot that's covered by buildings. It's calculated as the footprint of your house plus the footprint of any other roofed structures, divided by the total lot size. Your house is 810 square feet on a 4,800-square-foot lot, so you're at about 17% coverage. A 200-square-foot pergola would push you to about 21%. The R5 zone has a maximum building coverage that varies with lot size (Table 110-5); you have plenty of room. There's also a separate rule that *all detached accessory structures combined* cannot exceed 20% of the lot, which for you is about 960 square feet — way more than a pergola. (PCC 33.110.225 and 33.110.245.)

It absolutely matters whether the pergola is in the front yard or the back yard. The setbacks alone push it into the back: 10 feet from the front means a pergola right next to your front porch is generally not allowed. Beyond that, Portland's zoning code wants front yards to feel open, so accessory structures in front yards are heavily restricted. **Keep it behind the house** and you avoid almost all of these issues.

There's a useful exception worth knowing about: a small pergola **can encroach into the side or rear setbacks** (closer than 5 feet to those property lines) if it meets a specific list of conditions in PCC 33.110.245.C.2.b. Roughly: it has to be no more than 24 feet by 24 feet, no more than 15 feet tall overall, walls no more than 10 feet, screened by a fence or wall, with no doors or windows facing the neighbor, no rooftop deck, and at least 40 feet from the front lot line. A typical pergola easily clears those bars, so if you want to put it close to the back fence, you can — just keep it 3 feet from the line for the building code, which is the harder of the two limits.

## What to do next, in order

Here is the order I'd actually go in.

1. **Verify everything yourself on PortlandMaps.** Go to portlandmaps.com, type in 4773 SE 52nd Ave, and click around the "Permits & Zoning" and "Property" sections. This costs nothing and takes 15 minutes. You're double-checking the zone (R5) and the absence of overlays. This is the step where you catch anything I might have gotten wrong.

2. **Sketch a rough site plan.** Just paper and pencil is fine. Draw your lot to scale (50 feet wide on 52nd, 100 feet deep going east), mark the four property lines, sketch your house, and then draw where you want the pergola. Note the dimensions: how wide, how deep, how tall, how far from each property line. You'll need this for any permit conversation. Free, an afternoon.

3. **Schedule a free 15-minute appointment with PP&D.** Portland Permitting & Development offers free 15-minute virtual appointments with a planner specifically for questions like yours. This is the highest-leverage step in the whole process. Go to portland.gov/ppd/15-min or call 503-823-7300 to book it. Have your sketch ready, plus your address and your property ID (R336087, from the city's records). The planner can confirm in real time whether you need a zoning permit, a building permit, both, or neither. Free, 15 minutes plus scheduling time.

4. **If a tree is in play, call Urban Forestry.** If your pergola posts will be near a sizable tree, call Urban Forestry directly at 503-823-TREE (503-823-8733) before you commit to a design. They can tell you whether the tree counts as regulated, and whether you need a tree review. Free, 10 minutes.

5. **If no permit is needed, build it.** Source: lumber yard, weekend project, or hire a contractor. Make sure your contractor is licensed by the Oregon Construction Contractors Board (CCB), which you can verify at oregon.gov/ccb. Cost varies wildly; a DIY 12x12 cedar pergola runs roughly $1,500 to $3,500 in materials (cost of a decent home appliance), a contractor-built one $5,000 to $15,000 (cost of a used car).

6. **If a zoning permit is needed, apply online.** Go to portland.gov/ppd and look for residential permits. Upload your site plan, pay the fee, and wait for a planner to review and stamp it. Costs roughly $150 to $400 for a small project, takes one to three weeks typically.

7. **If a building permit is needed, the application is more involved.** You'll need a more detailed plan showing dimensions, post sizes, anchoring details, and footing depth. Same online portal. Fees on a $5,000-$10,000 project run roughly $300 to $700 depending on valuation, plus a 12% state surcharge that Oregon adds to all building, plumbing, electrical, and mechanical permits. Plan review typically takes two to four weeks for something this small.

8. **Schedule inspections if a building permit was issued.** You'll usually have a footing inspection (before you pour concrete in the post holes) and a final inspection (when you're done). The inspector is the friendly part; their job is to make sure it's safe, not to find reasons to fail you.

## What to actually say when you call PP&D

You can paste this straight into an email or read it on the phone.

"Hi, my name is [your name]. I own a single-family home at 4773 SE Fifty-Second Avenue in Portland, ZIP 97206. The property ID is R three three six zero eight seven, and I'm in the R Five zone with no overlays. I'd like to put up a freestanding pergola in my back yard. It would be about [X] feet by [Y] feet, no taller than [Z] feet, with an open lattice roof and no electrical or plumbing. It would be at least [N] feet from each property line. Can you confirm whether I need a zoning permit, a building permit, both, or neither for this project? And can you tell me whether there are any special site conditions I should know about, like trees, sewer easements, or right-of-way concerns?"

For email, send it to permits@portlandoregon.gov or use the contact form on portland.gov/ppd. They typically respond within a few business days.

If you talk to anyone about the trees specifically, here's a cleaner version of that:

"Hi, I'm planning to build a backyard pergola at 4773 SE Fifty-Second Avenue, property ID R three three six zero eight seven. I've got [a tree of about X inches diameter] on the property near where the pergola would go, and I want to make sure my post holes won't run into Title 11 issues. Can you walk me through what you'd need to see, and whether this rises to a tree review?"

## What people most often get wrong

These are the ones that actually trip up Portland homeowners on pergola projects. I'll give you the real-life version of each.

**Putting it too close to the property line.** Most people eyeball it. Their fence isn't always on the actual property line; sometimes it's a foot or two over. Before you set posts, get a survey, or at minimum measure carefully from any existing reference points (and assume you're wrong by a foot). The penalty for being too close is having to move it.

**Attaching to the house "just for stability" without realizing that triggers a permit.** A free-standing pergola near the house is one thing; bolting a ledger board to your siding is another. The bolt pattern, flashing, and load transfer all matter to the building code. If you want it close to the house, build it freestanding with its own posts unless you're prepared to permit it.

**Adding a solid roof "later."** Lots of homeowners build an open pergola, then a year later add corrugated panels or shingles to keep the rain off. That conversion turns the pergola into a "patio cover" under both Title 24 and PCC 33.110.245. The new impervious roof can also push you into stormwater rules (BES — the Bureau of Environmental Services, which runs sewers and stormwater — cares about anything that adds runoff). Best to decide on the roof before you build.

**Building over the sewer cleanout or a buried utility.** Your property has a sewer lateral running roughly south from the house out to the main line in SE Long Street, and there's a documented sewer backup history at the address. Setting a 4-foot-deep concrete post on top of a sewer line is a real way to ruin a Saturday. **Call 811** before you dig (Oregon Utility Notification Center, free, 48 hours notice required). They send out crews who mark gas, water, electric, cable, and sometimes sewer for free. This is the single most important thing on this whole list.

**Removing or seriously trimming a tree without checking.** Title 11 protects regulated trees. Even on your own property, taking down a 14-inch maple without a tree permit can result in mitigation fees in the thousands. The 2016 tree code permit on your property suggests you (or the previous owner) already went through this once. If anything is going to come out, talk to Urban Forestry first.

**Forgetting about easements.** Easements are legal rights other parties have to use part of your property — utility companies, the city, sometimes neighbors. Pergolas built on top of an easement can be required to come down. Your title report should list any easements; the city's records show none on your lot, which is a good sign, but verify with your closing documents from when you bought the place.

**Treating "no permit needed" as "no rules apply."** Permit-exempt structures still have to meet the building code, the zoning code, and the tree code. A neighbor complaint can trigger an inspection even after the fact, and "I didn't pull a permit" doesn't get you out of a code violation if the structure isn't compliant.

## Cost and timeline reality check

For a normal backyard pergola in your situation, here's what to plan around financially.

If you stay under all the permit thresholds (under 200 square feet, under 15 feet tall, freestanding, 3+ feet from property lines), permit fees can be **zero to about $200** for a quick zoning permit if one is needed. About the cost of a nice dinner out.

If you need a building permit because the pergola is bigger or attached, expect roughly **$300 to $700** in city fees for a project of $5,000-$10,000 valuation, plus the 12% state surcharge (so add another $35-$80). The minimum permit fee in Portland for FY 2025-26 is $153. About the cost of a nice power tool.

**System Development Charges** (the city's term for one-time fees the city charges for new construction that adds load to sewers, streets, parks, and water systems — abbreviated SDCs) generally **do not apply** to a pergola. SDCs are tied to new dwelling units (new houses, new ADUs, new apartments), not accessory structures like pergolas. The one edge case is if you add a lot of impervious roof area; very large solid-roof patio covers can trigger a small stormwater SDC from BES. An open-slat pergola in your size range will be exempt.

The **1% Affordable Housing Construction Excise Tax** kicks in only at $100,000 valuation or more, so you don't need to worry about it.

For timeline: a permit-exempt project is whenever you and the lumber are ready. A zoning permit usually clears in **one to three weeks**. A building permit for a small accessory structure typically clears in **two to four weeks** for the review, then there's the build itself plus inspections (usually two: footing and final, scheduled with about a week of lead time each). Roughly one summer's worth of weekends if you DIY.

## Friendly accuracy check

A few honest caveats so you don't go spend money on something I got wrong.

I pulled your zoning, lot size, and overlay status straight from PortlandMaps using the city's API, but **the city updates that data periodically**, and your situation could have changed. **Look it up yourself** at portlandmaps.com before you spend money on plans or materials. It takes five minutes and is the difference between being right and being mostly right.

Setback distances and permit thresholds in this answer reflect the current version of the Portland City Code as I read it today, but **codes get amended**, and the ones around residential infill, ADUs, and trees have been changing every couple of years. The version I cited (Title 33 Chapter 33.110, accessed via portland.gov/code/33) was current as of late 2024 / 2025. The 15-minute appointment with a PP&D planner exists specifically to give you the current answer, and it's free. Use it.

I'm a friendly explainer, not your zoning officer. If your project is borderline (bigger pergola, attached, near a tree, near a property line), get the question in writing from PP&D — usually an email reply from a planner is enough — before you build. That's the shield that protects you if a neighbor complains later.

If something I said about your specific lot doesn't match what you see on the ground (the property line is in a different place, there's a tree I didn't know about, the back fence is on a strange angle), trust your eyes and verify before building. The city's records are usually right but not always.

## Glossary

**BDS** — Bureau of Development Services, the old name for the city bureau that handled permits. Renamed PP&D in July 2024. You may still see "BDS" on older documents and signs.

**BES** — Bureau of Environmental Services, the city bureau that runs the sewers and stormwater system. Phone 503-823-7740.

**DBH** — Diameter at Breast Height, how the city measures tree trunks (about 4.5 feet off the ground).

**FAR** — Floor Area Ratio, the total floor area of all buildings on a lot divided by the lot size. Pergolas don't have enclosed floor area, so they don't count toward FAR.

**Lot coverage** — The share of your lot covered by buildings, measured as the footprint divided by the lot area.

**Overlay** — An extra set of rules layered on top of the base zoning, used for historic districts, environmental zones, design review areas, and certain neighborhoods. Your lot has none.

**PBOT** — Portland Bureau of Transportation, the bureau that owns streets and sidewalks. They get involved when you're working in the public right-of-way.

**PCC** — Portland City Code, the official rule book. Title 11 is trees. Title 24 is building regulations. Title 33 is zoning.

**PF&R** — Portland Fire & Rescue, the fire bureau. They mostly care about access and clearances around buildings, which doesn't typically come up for backyard pergolas.

**PP&D** — Portland Permitting & Development, the city bureau that handles building permits, zoning permits, tree permits, and inspections (formerly BDS, plus permit functions absorbed from Urban Forestry, BES, PBOT, and Water as of July 2024). 1900 SW Fourth Avenue, phone 503-823-7300.

**Regulated tree** — A tree the city tracks under Title 11. Private tree at 12+ inches DBH, City tree at 6+ inches, or street tree at 3+ inches.

**ROW** — Right-of-Way, the public strip along your property that the city owns (street, sidewalk, and the planting strip between them). You can't build on it.

**RPZ** — Root Protection Zone, the protected circle around a regulated tree. Roughly 1 foot of radius for every inch of trunk diameter.

**R5** — Residential 5,000 zone, single-family neighborhoods with minimum lots around 5,000 square feet. Your zone.

**SDC** — System Development Charge, the city's one-time fees for new construction that adds load to sewers, streets, parks, and water. Doesn't apply to typical pergolas.

**Setback** — The strip along each property line where you can't normally build. Your setbacks are 10 feet front, 5 feet sides, 5 feet rear.

**Urban Forestry** — The city group that handles tree code review, tree permits, and street trees. Phone 503-823-TREE (503-823-8733).

---

If you want, I can save this as a markdown file you can keep on your computer for the permit conversation, or I can sketch a more detailed version of the diagram showing exactly where you're thinking of putting the pergola if you tell me roughly where on the lot you want it. Happy to keep going.
